anti_claims: Semantic source of record remains core/doctrine_enrichment.json.
DOCTRINE.md and this JSON are projections and may be regenerated.
Local projection freshness does not imply the public website has been deployed.
A readable analogy or formal gloss does not prove correctness or support strength.
authority_boundary: Generated reader projection from core/doctrine_enrichment.json. It improves repository and agent navigability but is not source authority, proof authority, hosted publication, release approval, or permission to hand-edit generated surfaces.

agent.renders: bounded JSON packet from this reader projection

reference.renders: all records with all required facets plus digests

website.renders: site-specific HTML from the same enrichment model

quality_contract.one_fact_one_owner: A semantic fact has one owner: core/doctrine_enrichment.json. Markdown, CLI packets, website HTML, and reader JSON are typed projections.
quality_contract.semantic_parity_rule: Repository, agent, and website readers may render different profiles, but each must trace back to the same enrichment record and digest.

analogy.boundary: The stale-sign picture shows label-echo; it does not name the deeper rule, which is that the underlying evidence, not the sign, is what a verdict has to be re-derived from.

records.canonical_statement: Echoing a fixture verdict, registry label, or model answer as truth instead of recomputing it from lower-level evidence.
records.common_misread: That a label being present means it was checked. The label is just text until something recomputes the verdict beneath it.

counterexample.text: A dashboard shows a component as healthy because its own record said so, with no independent recomputation behind the green.

example.text: A monitor replay deliberately feeds a mislabelled trace and confirms the recomputed verdict rejects it, so a wrong label cannot pass by being repeated.

formal_reading.reads: Emitting a value v as a verdict while no checker recomputes v from the basis.

a declared label or verdict

records.governs: The claim boundary, where a label is mistaken for a derivation.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of repeating a stored verdict, label, or model answer as if it were true, without re-deriving it from the underlying evidence.
records.refuses: Repeating a fixture verdict, registry label, markdown claim, or model answer as if repetition were proof.
records.requires: Catching the echo: a recomputed verdict and a negative fixture that fails.
records.scope_boundary: Guarding against label echo is a floor under AX-1 and AX-12, not a positive proof that any specific claim is true.
records.significance: It is the most common way false confidence spreads: a single unverified label gets quoted as fact all the way downstream.
records.title: Fixture-label echo

analogy.boundary: This shows only that a familiar face is not the same as a confirmed permission. It does not prove the access system itself is correct, that every door in the building is checked this way, or that a confirmed person will behave well once inside.

records.canonical_statement: Trusting a producer, persona, route, credential, or prior success instead of a checker, proof, policy, or dereferenced capability.
records.common_misread: That a trusted name or a pass that worked before is a safe stand-in for checking. The check has to be made each time against what is actually allowed now.

counterexample.text: A mutation is allowed because the caller holds a long-lived credential and the same route passed last time, with nothing dereferencing current authority or recomputing the claim behind it.

example.text: The tool-authority replay derives whether a call is permitted from the dereferenced capability and policy rather than from the caller's standing identity, so a known caller with no derived authority is refused.

formal_reading.reads: Trusting a claim while no small checker accepts its certificate, or granting a subject an effect while authority for it was never derived by dereference from tokens, policy, and proof against world state.

a small checker that would decide the certificate

records.governs: The trust-and-authority boundary, where a producer's identity or a standing credential is mistaken for a check or a derived right.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of letting something through because of who sent it, what badge it carries, or that it worked last time, instead of independently checking that this request is actually allowed right now.
records.refuses: Trusting a producer, persona, route, credential, or prior success in place of a checker, proof, policy, or dereferenced capability.
records.requires: A small checker that decides the certificate, and authority for an effect derived by dereference from tokens, policy, and proof references against current world state.
records.scope_boundary: Refusing producer trust at these witnesses is a floor under AX-2 and AX-3, not positive proof that any granted authority or accepted claim is correct. The example is reader illustration and computes no support; catching identity-based trust in named places does not establish that authority is correctly derived everywhere.
records.significance: Identity is easy to fake or to go stale; a system that trusts the sender rather than re-checking the request looks fine until that sender is wrong or compromised, and by then nothing was actually guarding the door.
records.title: Producer trust

records.canonical_statement: Reading a count, a handle, a passing validator, or an evidence-class name as product maturity, release readiness, or whole-system correctness.
records.common_misread: That more passing checks always means more readiness. Extra passes only help if they cover a part that had no coverage before; repeating the same passing check adds nothing.

counterexample.text: A progress page reports that ninety checks pass and calls the system release-ready, with several required parts carrying no witness at all and the unwitnessed gaps averaged away instead of pinning the whole to blocked.

example.text: The coverage ledger projects how many declared parts are actually materialised and keeps a case that forbids reading an absent entry as covered, so a coverage tally is exposed as a bounded count rather than a completeness claim.

formal_reading.reads: Reporting a tally of passing parts as the standing of the whole while some part has no admissible witness, so the claimed status rises above the meet of the parts that an uncovered part should pin to the bottom.

symbols.meaning: reporting the count n as the standing of the whole
a count, tally, or rank reported as maturity
a passing part counted into the tally
a part of the composite (an obligation or component)

the meet over parts, the weakest part's status

records.governs: The progress-and-readiness boundary, where a summary number is tempted to stand in for the standing of the whole.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of reading a single summary number, like how many checks passed, as if it told you the whole thing is finished or ready, when parts you never measured could still be failing.
records.refuses: Treating counts, handles, passing validators, or evidence-class names as product maturity, release readiness, or whole-system correctness.
records.requires: Scoring a composite as the meet of its parts, defaulting an unwitnessed part to blocked, and raising standing only by covering an uncovered obligation, lifting a sub-maximal component, or adding a missing negative case.
records.scope_boundary: Refusing rank-as-score does not raise the standing of any part or make the composite correct. It only keeps the number honest as a projection of the ceiling; the parts beneath it carry exactly the support they were already shown to have, and an uncovered part stays blocked rather than counted.
records.significance: Counting passing checks as a maturity score quietly treats anything unmeasured as if it passed, so a project can look release-ready while a required piece was never even tested.
records.title: Rank-as-product-score

example.text: The source-import protocol keys imported material on a content digest and refuses to reuse a copy when the source digest no longer matches, so a drifted source cannot be served by its old path.

formal_reading.reads: Reusing a cached result by a stable handle when the digest of its basis now differs from the digest it was cached under.

records.governs: The reuse boundary, where a stable handle is mistaken for stable content.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of reusing a saved answer that is filed under a name or address, after the actual thing behind that name has been changed or replaced.
records.refuses: Serving a cached result by name, path, route, label, or producer after its basis content has changed.
records.requires: Keying reuse on a digest of the basis and recomputing or refusing when that digest drifts.
records.scope_boundary: Catching one stale-by-handle reuse at one digest boundary does not prove every cache in the system tracks its basis. This object and its example are reader illustration; they never count as support or proof that freshness holds across the graph, and the source records the freshness component as an open gap rather than a closed one.
records.significance: Pipelines reuse saved results by name to stay fast, but if the underlying source was edited, the name keeps returning yesterday's answer and nobody notices the value is stale.
records.title: Cache-across-drift

analogy.boundary: This shows only that 'we found nothing' is not the same as 'there is nothing'. It does not establish whether the un-imaged region is actually healthy or not, and it does not turn a properly scanned clear area into a guarantee.

maps.analogy: the region of the body that was never imaged
maps.doctrine: a missing witness, an evidence gap

maps.analogy: reading the un-imaged region as 'all clear'
maps.doctrine: reading that gap as a settled negative

maps.analogy: being able to say 'clear' only for the area actually scanned end to end
maps.doctrine: closing as a negative only inside a declared finite domain where every case is checked

analogy.text: A doctor reading a region of a scan as 'all clear' when that region was never actually imaged, so 'we saw no problem' gets reported as 'there is no problem'.

records.canonical_statement: Reading a missing witness as a settled negative outside a declared finite domain, so absence of proof is reported as proof of absence.
records.common_misread: That a blank or absence is the same as a confirmed 'nothing here'. Absence of evidence is unknown, not a settled negative, unless the whole area was genuinely covered.

counterexample.text: A coverage map shows an area as clear because no failing case was found there, with no declared finite domain behind the closure, so an unsearched region is printed as a settled negative.

example.text: The coverage ledger records what the system has not established as an open gap with a declared scope, and keeps a case that fails if a blank is read as a clean negative, so an unexamined area cannot be reported as proven-absent.

formal_reading.reads: Reporting the negation of a claim as established while the claim is merely unproven and the domain was never declared finite, so an open gap is laundered into a settled negative.

symbols.meaning: a claim whose proof is missing
reporting the negation as an established finding
an admissible derivation exists for the claim
D is a declared finite domain where closed-world coverage is valid
the domain the reasoning ranges over

records.governs: The coverage boundary, where a missing witness is tempted into a settled negative.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of treating a place where you found nothing as a settled finding that nothing is there, when you may simply not have looked, or not looked everywhere.
records.refuses: Treating absence of evidence as evidence of absence outside a declared closed domain.
records.requires: Holding an uncovered obligation as an open gap, and closing it as a negative only inside a declared finite domain.
records.scope_boundary: Holding a gap open as unknown does not establish the missing claim either way; it keeps the absence from becoming a false negative, and it grants no support, completeness, or coverage beyond the declared finite domain it names.
records.significance: An unexamined gap quietly reads as reassurance, so a coverage map can look finished when it has only stopped looking, hiding risk exactly where no one checked.
records.title: Unknown-unknown exhaustiveness

analogy.boundary: This shows only that a missing input should produce an explicit refusal, not a made-up answer. It does not prove that a dose computed with all the right inputs is correct, and a refusal is not itself a treatment.

maps.analogy: the missing patient weight the dose formula requires
maps.doctrine: a precondition that does not hold

maps.analogy: guessing and handing over a dose regardless
maps.doctrine: emitting a value anyway to keep going

maps.analogy: a written note saying 'dose not computed: weight missing' instead of a number
maps.doctrine: a typed refusal carrying its reason and evidence

analogy.text: A pharmacist guessing and dispensing a dose when the patient's weight, the one input the formula needs, is missing, instead of stopping and saying the dose cannot be worked out yet.

records.canonical_statement: Emitting a number or conclusion when the precondition fails or a dependency is missing, instead of a typed refusal that carries its reason and evidence.
records.common_misread: That returning some number is more helpful than refusing. A number produced when its inputs are missing is worse than a refusal, because it hides as a usable result.

counterexample.text: A statistics routine returns a coefficient computed past its valid sample size, and a downstream summary reports it as a normal result because it arrived as a number and not as a refusal.

example.text: The finance forecast evaluation component returns a typed refusal when a forecast horizon reaches or exceeds the sample size, or when the statistics dependency it needs is unavailable, rather than reporting a number computed outside its valid range.

formal_reading.reads: The precondition fails, yet the computation emits a value y instead of a refusal carrying a reason and its evidence.

symbols.meaning: the declared precondition the computation needs to hold
emitting the partial computation's value as a result
the partial computation on input x
an emitted number or conclusion
a typed refusal carrying a reason r and evidence e

records.governs: The boundary where a partial computation must either answer under its precondition or refuse.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of producing a number anyway when the thing it needs to be valid is missing, instead of clearly saying 'I cannot answer this and here is why'.
records.refuses: Emitting a meaningless statistic, a silent NaN, or an out-of-domain conclusion just to keep a green path.
records.requires: Catching the inadmissible value: a totalized result that returns Ok only under a declared precondition and otherwise a refusal carrying a reason and evidence.
records.scope_boundary: Refusing one inadmissible emission at a named site does not prove a computation total or its in-domain numbers correct. It keeps the absence of an answer honest; the value it does return is still only as strong as the basis and check beneath it, and this record and its example never count as support for that.
records.significance: A fabricated number looks just like a real one downstream, so a statistic computed outside its valid range slides into later steps that trust it, while an honest refusal stops the bad value at the door.
records.title: Inadmissible number emission

records.canonical_statement: Letting untrusted, private, provider, prompt, or raw-voice material reach a public or privileged sink without a carried provenance label and a declared sanitizing transform.
records.common_misread: That if the last copy step looks tidy and nothing obviously private is visible, the material is safe to publish.

counterexample.text: A public page prints a value lifted from a private-adjacent source because the final copy step looked clean, with no provenance carried across the copy and no sanitizing transform between the source and the page.

example.text: The prompt-injection replay carries an information-flow policy with the data, so an untrusted instruction arriving at a tool call is caught at the boundary instead of trusted for having reached a trusted endpoint.

formal_reading.reads: A flow carries x into a sink s while x's carried provenance label sits above what the sink's policy may admit, and the flow passes through no declared sanitizing transform whose output label the sink's policy admits.

a public or privileged sink
x's carried provenance class and ceiling
what the sink is allowed to admit
sits above the ceiling the sink admits
a declared sanitizing transform
T is a declared sanitizing transform whose output the sink's policy admits

records.governs: The boundary where material and its trust label cross into a public or privileged sink.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of letting private or untrusted material reach a public or high-trust place just because the last step looked clean, without a label saying where it came from and without running it through a stated cleaning step first.
records.refuses: Untrusted, private, provider, prompt, credential, or raw-voice material reaching a privileged or public sink on a clean-looking final step, with no provenance carried and no declared transform applied.
records.requires: Carrying a provenance class and ceiling with the value, and routing untrusted material through a declared transform the sink's policy admits before it crosses.
records.scope_boundary: Guarding the membrane at named endpoints is a floor under AX-8, not a positive proof of non-interference across the whole graph; endpoint catches are not general propagation, and the example illustrates the boundary rather than computing any support for it.
records.significance: It is how a system can publish from private-adjacent sources at all without leaking secrets, credentials, or planted instructions onto a public page.
records.title: Public/private membrane breach

records.canonical_statement: Performing an irreversible mutation with no compensator, no rollback record, no compare-and-swap guard, and no declared irreversible boundary.
records.common_misread: That because the normal case completes fine, you can always undo a change later if something goes wrong.

counterexample.text: A step writes directly over a shared path on the assumption the prior state still holds, with no compare-and-swap and no way to reverse it, so a concurrent write is silently overwritten.

example.text: The work-landing replay refuses to advance when the expected parent has moved under it and when the head was not advanced as recorded, so a multi-step effect cannot land blindly over a state that has already changed.

formal_reading.reads: An effect e is landed while no compensator reverses it, no irreversible boundary is declared for it, and no compare-and-swap guards it against the parent state having advanced.

an effect being written to the world
a compensator that would reverse the effect

records.governs: The effect boundary, where a mutation is treated as safely landed.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of making a real, hard-to-undo change while assuming it can always be taken back, when nothing was set up to take it back and nothing checked the situation had not already moved.
records.refuses: Writing an irreversible effect with no compensator, no rollback record, no compare-and-swap or world-version guard, and no declared irreversible boundary.
records.requires: Catching the blind write: a compensator or a declared irreversible boundary, compare-and-swap against the expected parent for multi-step effects, and a single writer per path.
records.scope_boundary: Guarding the named landing paths against blind writes does not prove every effect in the system is reversible or race-free. It holds the specific obligations AX-9 names, compensator-or-boundary, compare-and-swap, single-writer, over the surfaces that bind them, and the example illustrates that floor rather than counting as support for it.
records.significance: It stops a change from silently overwriting someone else's concurrent work or landing half-done with no recorded way to undo it.
records.title: Blind irreversible mutation

records.canonical_statement: Freezing a count, a "current" state, or any live value into durable prose with no recorded time, basis, or way to re-derive it.
records.common_misread: That because the number was true the moment it was written down, it stays a reliable fact afterward.

counterexample.text: A page states "the system has 77 components" as a flat fact; the figure was right when typed, but nothing records when it was read or how to recompute it, and it keeps reading as current long after it drifts.

example.text: The fact-claim audit treats a volatile count written without a freshness contract as a blocking condition rather than a fact, so a frozen live number is caught instead of trusted because it once was true.

formal_reading.latex: \mathrm{durable}(\varphi) \;\wedge\; \mathrm{live}(\varphi) \;\wedge\; \neg\,\exists\, a, b, r.\ \mathrm{carries}(\varphi, \langle a, b, r \rangle)
formal_reading.reads: A live-state value is frozen into durable prose while no as-of time, basis, and rederive route travel with it.

records.governs: The time-and-freshness boundary, where a moving value is written down as if it were fixed.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of writing a moving number or a 'current' status into permanent text as if it were a fixed fact, with no note of when it was read, what it was read from, and no way to recompute it.
records.refuses: Freezing a count, a "current" state, or a live total into prose with no as-of, basis, or way to re-derive it.
records.requires: Catching the freeze: a live value must carry an as-of time, a basis, and a rederive route, stay out of durable prose, or be marked a dated snapshot.
records.scope_boundary: Blocking unbound live values does not prove a freshness contract is in place everywhere, nor that any carried as-of value is current. It is a floor under AX-10 and P-11; it catches the frozen figure, it does not certify that the figures still bound to a basis are fresh.
records.significance: It keeps readers from trusting a stale figure that reads exactly like a live one, long after the real number has moved.
records.title: Frozen live fact

records.canonical_statement: Granting executable authority to prose doctrine that is not accepted by the grammar and carries no result-record obligation or scope limit.
records.common_misread: That a clearly and authoritatively worded statement is, by virtue of how it reads, a rule the system enforces.

example.text: The executable doctrine grammar accepts a standard as authority only when it parses in the grammar and carries its obligation and scope-limit fields, and keeps a negative case where a prose-only standard that overclaims completeness is rejected rather than admitted.

formal_reading.reads: If an artifact is granted governing authority while it is not accepted by the doctrine grammar, or carries no result-record obligations, or carries no scope limit, reject the authority and treat the prose as a projection.

records.governs: The boundary between doctrine that governs and prose that only reads as if it does.
records.plain_meaning: The mistake of treating a confidently written paragraph as a binding rule, when the system never accepted it through its formal checks and it never says what evidence it owes or where it does not apply.
records.refuses: Granting executable authority to prose doctrine without grammar membership, result-record obligations, and a scope limit.
records.requires: Refusing authority to any doctrine that is not accepted by the grammar or lacks declared result-record obligations and a stated scope limit, holding it as a projection instead.
records.scope_boundary: Rejecting prose-as-authority is a floor under AX-11, not a positive proof that any admitted standard is correct or complete. It keeps unparsed prose from governing; a standard that does pass the grammar is still only as strong as the obligations it actually carries, and this example illustrates the rule without counting as support for it.
records.significance: It stops a pile of confident-sounding paragraphs from accumulating as if they were enforced rules nothing can actually check.
records.title: Prose-as-executable-authority

maps.doctrine: missing evidence defaults to blocked

analogy.text: A plane does not take off because most of the pre-flight checklist passed. One required check left blank keeps it at the gate.

records.canonical_statement: A composite is only as trusted as its weakest part, missing evidence defaults to blocked rather than passing, and a later stage may lower authority but never raise it without a fresh derivation.
records.common_misread: That enough green parts can outvote a blocked one. They cannot; the weakest required part sets the ceiling.

counterexample.text: A status board marks a composite green because no part reported a failure, treating the absence of a recorded check as if it were a passing one.

example.text: The release wording gate runs over the system's own public copy and stops the export when it finds overclaiming language, so a later packaging stage cannot raise the claim above what the evidence beneath it supports.

formal_reading.reads: A composite's status is the meet of its parts' statuses; every part with no evidence is blocked; and a later status may exceed the earlier one only when some fresh derivation establishes it.

symbols.meaning: the trust status of a composite c
the meet (greatest lower bound) taken over the parts
the parts the composite is built from
a part of the composite
the evidence available for part p
bottom: blocked, the default when a part's evidence is absent
a later status proposed for the same composite
strictly higher in the trust order than
a fresh derivation
derivation d establishes the higher status

records.governs: The default status of missing evidence and the direction trust is allowed to move across stages.
records.plain_meaning: Something built from parts is only as trusted as its weakest required part. A part with no evidence counts as blocked, not as fine, and a later step can lower trust but never quietly raise it.
records.refuses: A silent default pass.
records.requires: Taking the meet of part statuses, defaulting an unwitnessed part to blocked, and a fresh derivation before any stage raises authority.
records.scope_boundary: Defaulting to blocked and refusing silent upgrades does not prove any composite actually correct or complete; it fixes the safe direction of trust, and an obligation with no admissible witness stays blocked rather than becoming true by default.
records.significance: It makes a gap the safe state, so a release that finds private-adjacent content or overclaiming language stops instead of shipping.
records.title: Fail-closed monotone lattice

analogy.boundary: The drawer picture works only when the search space is small and fully declared, like one drawer; it does not let you call something false across an open world you never finished searching.

maps.analogy: the one drawer you searched all the way through
maps.doctrine: the declared finite domain with complete coverage

maps.analogy: the rest of the house you have not checked
maps.doctrine: the unknown verdict outside it

analogy.text: Not finding your keys in one drawer only tells you they are not in that drawer. It does not tell you they are gone.

records.canonical_statement: Not proving a claim is not proving its negation; an absence becomes a negative only inside a declared finite domain whose coverage is complete, and otherwise stays unknown.
records.common_misread: That no evidence means no problem. Outside a fully checked list, no evidence just means not yet known.

counterexample.text: A status surface reports a property as clean because a scan returned no findings, without ever declaring the finite domain the scan covered, so silence over an unbounded space is read as a guarantee.

example.text: The self-ignorance coverage ledger tracks the gap between what has been declared in scope and what has actually been materialized, and forbids the inference that an unmapped region is empty; the open space stays a recorded, computable gap rather than a quiet claim of completeness.

formal_reading.reads: Only when a claim ranges inside a domain that is declared finite and completely covered does failing to prove it license concluding its negation; in every other case the verdict on an unproven claim is unknown, never refuted.

symbols.meaning: a claim under consideration
the domain the claim ranges over
a declared domain
the domain is explicitly declared finite (closed-world)
coverage over D is complete, so nothing in it was left unexamined
the claim has not been proven
the verdict the system records for the claim
the open-world verdict, distinct from refuted

records.governs: Whether an absence of evidence is allowed to count as a negative result, and inside what scope.
records.plain_meaning: Not finding evidence for something is not the same as showing it is false. You can only call an absence a 'no' inside a list you have fully checked; otherwise the honest answer is 'unknown'.
records.refuses: Absence of evidence treated as evidence of absence.
records.requires: An explicitly declared finite domain with complete coverage before any closed-world conclusion; outside it, an unproven claim yields unknown rather than its negation.
records.scope_boundary: Tracking a declared-versus-materialized gap does not exhaust the space of unknown unknowns or prove the unmapped region empty. The open-world unknown it preserves is a coverage state, not a support verdict, and it never certifies that what was not found does not exist.
records.significance: It stops the system turning 'we did not see a problem' into 'there is no problem', which is how silent failures get signed off.
records.title: Open-world epistemics

analogy.boundary: The cash machine shows that a failed operation must decline with a reason instead of emitting a stray figure; it does not establish that every operation in the system declines this way, and untested failure paths can still leak a bad value rather than a clean refusal.

maps.analogy: the conditions the machine needs to release cash, like a valid balance and a reachable account
maps.doctrine: the precondition that must hold for a real result

maps.analogy: the machine dispensing the requested amount when everything checks out
maps.doctrine: Ok carrying a value when the precondition holds

maps.analogy: the printed decline notice naming why it stopped
maps.doctrine: a typed refusal carrying a reason and evidence

maps.analogy: never handing out an arbitrary stack of cash that you'd treat as a real withdrawal
maps.doctrine: never a silent number or NaN leaking downstream

analogy.text: A cash machine that can't complete a withdrawal prints a stated decline reason and gives you nothing, rather than spitting out some arbitrary amount of cash that you'd carry off as if it were correct.

records.canonical_statement: A partial computation returns a real answer only under its declared precondition; outside it, it returns a typed refusal carrying a reason and evidence, never a silent number or NaN.
records.common_misread: That a number coming out the end means the conditions were fine, so a calculation that quietly produced a value can be treated as a valid answer without checking it had grounds to run.

counterexample.text: A statistics routine divides by a sample size that is too small and returns the resulting NaN as if it were a score, and a downstream page prints it as a real forecast number.

example.text: The finance forecast evaluation spine returns a typed refusal naming the reason when a forecast horizon is at or above the sample size, or when the statistics dependency needed for a t-distribution is unavailable, instead of returning a number computed outside its domain.

formal_reading.latex: \forall\, x.\ \big(\mathrm{Pre}(x) \Rightarrow \exists\, y.\ g(x) = \mathrm{Ok}(y)\big) \;\wedge\; \big(\neg\,\mathrm{Pre}(x) \Rightarrow \exists\, r, e.\ g(x) = \mathrm{Refusal}(r, e)\big)
formal_reading.reads: For every input: when the declared precondition holds, the computation returns Ok carrying some value; when it does not hold, the computation returns a typed Refusal carrying a reason and the evidence behind it. Both branches are total; there is no third, silent outcome.

symbols.meaning: a partial computation, totalized into a Result
an input to the computation
the declared precondition on input x
a successful result carrying value y
a typed refusal carrying a reason r and evidence e

records.governs: What a partial computation is allowed to return when its precondition does not hold.
records.plain_meaning: A calculation may return a real number only when the conditions it needs actually hold. When they don't, it must hand back a clearly labelled refusal that names the reason and the evidence, never a quiet number or a blank that the next step reads as an answer.
records.refuses: An inadmissible number or silent NaN returned where no precondition was checked.
records.requires: A declared precondition for the Ok path and a typed Refusal carrying a reason and evidence on every other path, including missing dependencies and undefined values.
records.scope_boundary: Returning typed refusals at these named computations does not prove that every partial path in the system is totalized. It covers the declared preconditions and dependency gaps that are actually checked; uncovered partial functions remain a gap rather than a guaranteed refusal.
records.significance: It means a missing input or an out-of-range condition shows up as a visible, routable gap instead of a plausible-looking figure that a later page prints as a genuine result.
records.title: Typed partiality and refusal

analogy.boundary: The kitchen-label picture shows that the tag must travel and be transformed on purpose; it does not capture the full tracing of how information flows and combines, which is stricter than one label.

maps.analogy: the allergen tag that travels with the ingredient
maps.doctrine: the provenance label and ceiling carried with the value

maps.analogy: the documented step that removes or contains the allergen before serving
maps.doctrine: the declared transform before a sensitive output

analogy.text: Allergen information has to follow an ingredient all the way through the kitchen. A clean-looking label on the final plate is not enough if nobody tracked the nuts.

records.canonical_statement: Provenance labels and ceilings travel with a value across every boundary; untrusted material reaches a privileged sink only through a declared transform.
records.common_misread: That a clean label at the end shows a clean path. Only a label that actually travelled, with a declared cleaning step, counts.

counterexample.text: A public page prints a value lifted from a private-adjacent source because the final copy step looked clean, with no provenance carried across the copy.

example.text: The prompt-injection replay carries the information-flow policy with the data, so untrusted instructions arriving at a tool call are caught at the boundary instead of trusted because they reached a trusted endpoint.

formal_reading.reads: A derived value carries at least the restriction of what it was derived from; and for any flow into a sink, either the value's label already sits within the sink's policy, or the flow passes through a declared transform whose output label the sink's policy admits. The endpoint never stamps the label; the label arrives with the value.

symbols.meaning: a value entering a flow
a value derived from it along the flow
a processing step along the flow
a sink (a public or privileged output)
the value's provenance class and how far it may travel
what the sink is allowed to admit
a declared sanitising transform the flow passes through
the transform is declared, not stamped at the door

records.governs: How material and its trust label move across boundaries.
records.plain_meaning: Where a value came from, and how far it is allowed to travel, must follow it everywhere. Untrusted material can only reach a sensitive place after passing through a stated cleaning step.
records.refuses: An endpoint asserting a clean label without that label having propagated.
records.requires: A provenance class and ceiling carried with the value, and a declared transform before untrusted material reaches a privileged sink.
records.scope_boundary: Catching labelled flows at named endpoints does not prove non-interference across the whole graph. General propagation is bounded by what is actually traced, and the registry records that debt rather than hiding it.
records.significance: It blocks private, provider, or unverified input quietly reaching a public or privileged output just because the final step looked clean.
records.title: Provenance propagation and non-interference

analogy.boundary: This only describes when a change is allowed to be called done; it does not show that every change in the system can be undone, and one transfer that passed its checks does not establish that all transfers are reversible or collision-free.

maps.analogy: a transfer treated as completed
maps.doctrine: an effect treated as landed

maps.analogy: the bank's ability to reverse the transfer
maps.doctrine: a declared compensator that can reverse the effect

maps.analogy: the transfer marked final and non-refundable
maps.doctrine: a declared irreversible boundary

maps.analogy: the starting balance being unchanged when the leg runs
maps.doctrine: the compare-and-swap expected-parent check still holding

maps.analogy: no second transfer touching the same account at the same time
maps.doctrine: the single-writer constraint on the path

analogy.text: A money transfer between accounts only completes if the bank can either reverse it or has marked it as final and non-refundable, and a multi-leg transfer only proceeds when the balance it started from has not changed and no second transfer is touching the same account.

records.canonical_statement: An effect may count as landed only with a compensator or a declared irreversible boundary; multi-step effects land as a saga under compare-and-swap and single-writer constraints.
records.common_misread: Reading this as a promise that everything is reversible. It actually allows changes to be marked permanently final too; the rule is that the change must declare which one it is, not that undo is always possible.

counterexample.text: A step writes to a shared path and is treated as landed because the call returned, with no expected-parent check and no single-writer constraint, so two concurrent writers interleave and the final state matches neither.

example.text: The mission transaction work spine lands a multi-step effect only when its expected parent still matches the head, so a run whose head advanced underneath it fails with a parent-mismatch rather than overwriting the newer state.

formal_reading.reads: An effect may count as landed only if it declares a compensator or an irreversible boundary; and a multi-step effect lands only while the parent it expected still matches the head and at most one writer holds its path. Reversibility is not promised; what is required is that every effect declares which kind it is.

symbols.meaning: the effect e is treated as landed
a declared compensator that can reverse e
a declared irreversible boundary for e
the effect e is a multi-step effect
the compare-and-swap expected-parent check still holds
the single-writer constraint on e's path

records.governs: When a mutation is allowed to count as landed.
records.plain_meaning: A change to the world only counts as done if there is a stated way to undo it, or a clear note saying it can never be undone. A multi-step change only goes through if the starting point it expected is still there and nobody else is changing the same thing at the same time.
records.refuses: A blind irreversible write.
records.requires: A declared compensator or an irreversible boundary, and for multi-step effects a compare-and-swap expected-parent check plus a single-writer constraint on the path.
records.scope_boundary: Blocking parent-mismatch and same-path conflict on these named paths does not prove every effect in the system is compensable or every concurrent write is serialized. The legacy strong label is not a computed support verdict, and the routing registry still records that mapping these negative cases to a full rejection of the blind irreversible write remains unverified.
records.significance: It stops a half-finished change from being silently treated as complete, which is how a system ends up in a state nobody chose and nobody can walk back.
records.title: Compensable transactional effects

analogy.boundary: This only covers when a live number can still be stated as current; it does not establish that any particular reading is right now, and a reading that carries its date is not therefore fresh today.

maps.analogy: a number describing ongoing electricity usage
maps.doctrine: a claim over live, moving state

maps.analogy: the figure shown on the meter
maps.doctrine: the value asserted

maps.analogy: the date the reading was recorded
maps.doctrine: the as-of time the value was taken

maps.analogy: which meter the reading came from
maps.doctrine: the basis the value was read from

maps.analogy: the procedure for taking a fresh reading
maps.doctrine: the rule that recomputes the value

analogy.text: An electricity meter reading is only useful on a bill if it records the date it was taken, which meter it came from, and how to take a fresh reading; the bare number alone tells you nothing about today's usage.

records.canonical_statement: A claim about live state is a cached read, valid only while it carries when it was taken, what it was read from, and how to recompute it.
records.common_misread: Thinking that attaching a date and a source makes the number current. It only makes the number checkable; whether it still holds has to be read again from the source.

counterexample.text: A page prints a running total once and leaves it on display for weeks, with no as-of time and no rule to recompute it, so a stale snapshot reads as a live figure.

example.text: The fact-claim audit flags a volatile numeric claim that carries no freshness binding as a stable blocking code, so a live count cannot be published as if it were still current without declaring when it was taken and how to recompute it.

formal_reading.latex: \mathrm{valid}(\varphi, t') \;\Rightarrow\; \varphi = \langle v, t, B, \rho \rangle \;\wedge\; \big(\mathrm{holds}(B, t, t') \;\vee\; v = \rho(B)\big)
formal_reading.reads: A live-state claim may still be stated as current at a time of use only when it carries its value, its read time, its basis, and its rederive rule, and either the basis is unchanged between reading and use or the value has been freshly recomputed by that rule.

symbols.meaning: the claim may still be stated as current at use time t'
a claim over live state
the value asserted
the time the value was read
the later time the claim is used
the basis the value was read from
the rule that recomputes the value from the basis
the declared basis is unchanged between the read and the use

records.governs: When a value read from live state is still allowed to be stated as current.
records.plain_meaning: A number about something that keeps changing is just a reading taken at a moment, not a permanent fact. To be usable later it has to say when it was read, where it was read from, and how to read it again.
records.refuses: A frozen live count stated without a refresh basis.
records.requires: A value carrying its as-of time, its basis, and a rederive rule, with invalidation or recomputation once that basis no longer holds.
records.scope_boundary: Blocking unbound volatile numbers does not make any specific number current or correct, and it does not establish that staleness is caught everywhere. The freshness component of this axiom's own ceiling stays an ordered unknown until a source-owned refresh contract exists, so this object names the obligation rather than certifying that it is met.
records.significance: It keeps a number that was true once from quietly being read as if it were still true weeks later, after the thing it measured has moved on.
records.title: Temporal validity and freshness contracts

analogy.boundary: This only governs whether a rule is allowed to bind at all; passing the format check does not show the rule is complete or correct, and a cleared declaration is not therefore an accurate account of the goods.

maps.analogy: a declaration that clears the goods
maps.doctrine: a doctrine artifact carrying governing authority

maps.analogy: passing the required customs form check
maps.doctrine: membership in the executable grammar

maps.analogy: the supporting paperwork that must travel with it
maps.doctrine: the evidence obligations the artifact must carry

maps.analogy: the declaration's stated exclusions, what it does not vouch for
maps.doctrine: the declared statement of what it does not establish

maps.analogy: the convincing letter that clears nothing
maps.doctrine: prose held only as a readable projection

analogy.text: A customs declaration only clears goods when it passes the required form check, arrives with its supporting paperwork, and states plainly what it does not vouch for; a politely worded letter describing the shipment, however convincing, clears nothing.

records.canonical_statement: A doctrine artifact may claim authority only when an executable grammar accepts its shape and it carries receipt obligations and an anti-claim; prose alone is a projection.
records.common_misread: Assuming that passing the format check means the rule fully covers its subject. Passing the check admits the rule's shape only; it never certifies that what the rule says is complete.

counterexample.text: A persuasive standard written in prose is treated as binding because it reads authoritatively, with no grammar accepting its shape and no anti-claim or receipt obligations carried.

example.text: The executable doctrine grammar component accepts a standard only when its shape parses and its receipt obligations and anti-claim are present, and rejects a prose standard that asserts authority without that executable form.

formal_reading.reads: A doctrine artifact carries authority only when an executable grammar accepts its shape and it presents its evidence obligations and its own anti-claim; an artifact failing any of these sits in the projection tier, which can be read but cannot govern.

symbols.meaning: the doctrine artifact d carries governing authority
a doctrine artifact (standard, contract, rule)
the language an executable grammar G accepts
the result-record obligations the artifact must carry
the artifact's declared statement of what it does not prove
the projection tier, below authority

records.governs: Whether a doctrine artifact may govern, or is only a readable projection of one.
records.plain_meaning: A rule is only allowed to govern when it passes a fixed format check and comes with the evidence it must produce and a clear statement of what it does not cover. A well-written paragraph on its own is just something to read, not a rule.
records.refuses: Prose claiming executable authority.
records.requires: Acceptance by an executable grammar plus carried receipt obligations and a declared anti-claim, not well-formed prose.
records.scope_boundary: A grammar accepting an artifact's shape does not prove the doctrine it expresses is complete or correct; admitting the form is not certifying the coverage, and this reader page is itself a projection that cannot raise the authority of the source it explains.
records.significance: It stops a confident-sounding paragraph from being treated as an enforced rule just because it reads authoritatively, keeping unchecked prose out of the seat of governance.
records.title: Executable grammar before doctrine authority

analogy.boundary: The referee picture shows no special pleading; it does not say the system is therefore complete or correct, only that it cannot exempt itself.

maps.analogy: the referee judging their own team
maps.doctrine: Microcosm's own claims about itself

maps.analogy: the single rulebook that applies to everyone
maps.doctrine: the same admissibility floor as external claims

maps.analogy: the referee's own match report, checked like any other
maps.doctrine: the pages that describe the system, held to the floor they describe

analogy.text: A referee cannot switch to an easier rulebook when the call is about their own team. The same rules apply to them.

records.canonical_statement: Every claim Microcosm makes about itself passes the same admissibility floor it imposes on everything else; the meta-layer gets no exemption.
records.common_misread: That applying the rule to itself makes the system trustworthy by default. It only removes the exemption; the limits remain, stated rather than hidden.

counterexample.text: A system publishes a self-assessment of "verified and complete" that would never pass the evidence floor it applies to the work it audits.

example.text: The release wording gate runs the same claim-language floor over Microcosm's own public copy that it imposes on any other shard, blocking overclaiming language in the system's own pages.

formal_reading.reads: For every Microcosm artifact and every claim it carries, the claim is admitted under the same relation as an external claim.

symbols.meaning: the Microcosm artifacts, including its own pages and validators
any such artifact
a claim the artifact carries
the claims the artifact carries
the admissibility floor from AX-1
the same admissibility relation applied to an external claim

records.governs: Whether the system's own outputs get a discount.
records.plain_meaning: The system holds its own statements about itself to the same standard it holds everyone else's. It gets no discount for being the one doing the checking.
records.refuses: A meta-artifact exemption.
records.requires: That meta artifacts, pages, validators, records, and projections, meet the same evidence and refusal floor as everything else.
records.scope_boundary: Applying the floor to its own artifacts does not make the system complete or correct. It only removes the special pleading; the limits remain, stated rather than hidden.
records.significance: It lets the system state its own limits without that statement quietly becoming a new unchecked claim.
records.title: Reflexive accountability / no privileged meta-layer

analogy.boundary: The re-marking picture shows recomputation plus a trap for false passes; it does not capture every kind of evidence a real check runs over.

maps.analogy: the exam paper itself, worked from scratch
maps.doctrine: the evidence underneath the verdict

maps.analogy: re-working the answer instead of copying the key
maps.doctrine: recomputing the verdict from the evidence

maps.analogy: the planted wrong answer the marking must catch
maps.doctrine: the negative case the checker must reject

analogy.text: A teacher re-works the exam answer from scratch instead of copying the answer key, and also keeps a wrong-answer version to confirm the marking actually catches mistakes.

records.canonical_statement: Recompute a verdict from lower-level evidence instead of repeating a label, and keep a negative fixture where the cheap lie would slip through.
records.common_misread: That re-checking once makes a result permanently safe. It only earns trust for that result, against that check.

counterexample.text: A summary repeats a component's self-reported "passed" into a status board without ever recomputing it or keeping a case that would fail.

example.text: The benchmark-integrity replay recomputes a score from the run rather than trusting the reported number, and keeps gaming attempts as negative cases that must be caught.

formal_reading.reads: Trust a verdict only when a checker recomputes it from the underlying evidence and that same checker rejects the negative fixture built to slip the cheap lie through.

symbols.meaning: a builder is willing to trust the verdict on a claim
the verdict being claimed
the verdict a checker recomputes from the basis
the lower-level evidence the checker runs over
a negative fixture built so the cheap lie should fail
the checker's refusal on that negative fixture

records.governs: The claim boundary, before a result is allowed to mean anything.
records.plain_meaning: Trust a result only after re-deriving it from the underlying evidence, and keep a deliberately wrong test case that the check is required to reject.
records.refuses: Echoing a declared verdict, label, or count as if repetition made it true.
records.requires: Recomputation from lower-level evidence plus a negative fixture that the checker rejects.
records.scope_boundary: Recomputing one verdict from evidence does not make the principle a witness for AX-1; it operationalises specific obligations and inherits no authority beyond them.
records.significance: Without it, a label that says 'passed' gets repeated down the line and nobody ever re-derives whether it was true.
records.title: Recompute, do not echo

analogy.boundary: The blood-test picture shows that a result is limited to what was measured; it does not show how several separate checks are combined, where the overall ceiling drops to the weakest one. It is an illustration, not support that the rule holds everywhere.

maps.analogy: the iron test only supports a statement about iron
maps.doctrine: the claim's strength is capped at what the named check decides

maps.analogy: writing 'perfectly healthy' off one narrow blood test
maps.doctrine: prose overreaching past the check into system-wide authority

analogy.text: A blood test that measures one thing, like iron levels, tells you about iron. It does not let you write 'this person is in perfect health' on the chart.

records.canonical_statement: A claim may be no stronger than what its named checker actually decides; a checker that settles a local contract cannot license prose that projects global authority.
records.common_misread: That because the underlying check was real, any confident sentence built on it is also safe. The check sets a ceiling; words above that ceiling are not earned and do not prove the wider claim.

counterexample.text: A component validates that a result record has the expected fields, and a summary page reads that shape check as evidence the underlying property holds across the whole system.

example.text: The formal-math readiness gate computes how far its available evidence actually reaches and reports that boundary as the claim, refusing to narrate a local check into a proof of readiness.

formal_reading.reads: A claim's strength is bounded above by the meet of the strengths of exactly the checks that actually decide it; the ceiling is the weakest deciding component, not the most impressive one.

symbols.meaning: the strength a claim is allowed to assert
the claim being made
a checker, validator, registry, or witness route that decides part of the claim
the basis the deciding checks run over
meet: the weakest of the deciding components
the set of checks that actually settle the claim, excluding ones it merely names

records.governs: The strength boundary, where a claim is sized to the check that accepts it.
records.plain_meaning: A statement can only be as strong as the exact thing the check actually settled. If a check confirms one small, local fact, the writing around it must not stretch that into a sweeping claim about the whole system.
records.refuses: Letting a checker that decides only a local contract license prose that projects global or system-wide authority.
records.requires: Capping a claim at the meet of what its named checker, validator, registry, or witness route actually computes, with the ceiling derived from the grounding obligations rather than asserted.
records.scope_boundary: Holding a claim to its checker's strength does not raise that checker, and the principle is not a witness for AX-1 or AX-5; it operationalises specific grounding obligations and inherits no support beyond the covers computed for them. The worked example illustrates the rule for a reader and never counts as support that the principle is obligation-backed or complete.
records.significance: It stops a narrow, genuine check from being written up as a broad system-wide claim, so readers can size a claim to what was really tested.
records.title: Lower claim strength to checker strength

analogy.boundary: The weighing-plate picture shows where the deciding power sits and that the producer is kept out; it does not show that a small checker is automatically a correct or complete one. A simple checker can still be wrong, and this is only an illustration.

maps.analogy: the weighing plate that re-checks each item
maps.doctrine: the small checker that rereads and decides

maps.analogy: the shopper, who is not allowed to confirm their own bag
maps.doctrine: the large producer kept outside the trusted decision

analogy.text: At a self-checkout, the small weighing plate re-weighs each item to confirm what was scanned. The shopper, who knows what is in the bag, is never the one who decides the bag is correct.

records.canonical_statement: Put trust in a small checker that rereads a certificate and decides, and keep the large producer outside that authority boundary.
records.common_misread: That a confident, capable producer can be trusted to vouch for itself. Once the thing being judged also decides, there is no real check left, only restated confidence.

counterexample.text: A large model is trusted because it sounds sure of its answer, with no small separate checker rereading anything, so the producer is also the judge.

example.text: The certificate kernel lab keeps producers separate from the checker: a producer emits certificate material and a small kernel rereads and decides, so the authority lives in the part you can inspect and rerun rather than in the producer.

formal_reading.reads: Trust a verdict only when a small checker rereads a certificate and accepts, the trusted base consists of that checker alone, and the producer that emitted the certificate sits outside that base.

symbols.meaning: a builder is willing to trust the verdict on a claim
the claim being trusted
the small checker that decides
the certificate the producer emits
the trusted base the claim rests on: the set of components you must believe
the large component that emitted the certificate, kept outside the trusted base
is kept outside

records.governs: Where trust is concentrated, between the large producer and the small thing that decides.
records.plain_meaning: Put your trust in one small, simple step that re-does the work and decides, while letting the big complicated thing that produced the answer stay outside that decision. The thing being judged should not get to be its own judge.
records.refuses: Trusting a large producer's own confidence in place of a small, inspectable, rerunnable check.
records.requires: A small checker that rereads a certificate and accepts, with the producer kept outside the trusted boundary.
records.scope_boundary: Concentrating trust in a small checker does not make the principle a witness for AX-2, and a small checker is not automatically a correct or complete one. It operationalises specific obligations and inherits no authority beyond them; admission, support, and this reader illustration stay independent.
records.significance: Keeping the deciding part small means the part you actually have to audit and re-run stays small, even as the systems producing answers grow large and hard to inspect.
records.title: Concentrate trust in small checkers

analogy.boundary: The bank-transfer picture shows that holding a credential is not the same as being cleared to act; it does not capture everything weighed at decision time, such as the specific permissions, the rules, and the side-effect records. It is an illustration only.

maps.analogy: merely having the card number
maps.doctrine: the standing credential or role name that is not authority on its own

maps.analogy: the bank deciding each payment against this account, right now
maps.doctrine: authority derived per action from policy and current world state

analogy.text: Knowing someone's bank card number does not let money move. For each payment, the bank checks this card, this amount, and the live account state before it lets the transfer go through.

records.canonical_statement: Authority to mutate is derived from dereferenced proof, policy, and current world state; a credential, role name, or trusted-session phrasing authorizes nothing on its own.
records.common_misread: That a valid credential is enough to act. The credential is one input; the actual change still has to be cleared for that exact action against the current state.

counterexample.text: A mutation is allowed because the caller holds a valid admin credential, with nothing dereferencing that credential to the specific effect or checking it against policy and current state.

example.text: The tool-authority replay binds each tool effect to a declared scope and approval and rejects an overbroad scope or an unapproved side effect, so reach is derived from declared authority rather than from the tool being callable.

formal_reading.reads: Authorization for a subject and an effect is the output of the derivation over the dereferenced tokens, governing policy, proof references, and world state; ambient identity whose tokens dereference to nothing grants nothing. The credential is an input to the derivation, never a substitute for it.

symbols.meaning: a subject is authorized for an effect
a subject requesting an effect
the effect or mutation it wants to land
the function deriving authorization from the dereferenced tokens, policy, proofs, and world state

current world state

records.governs: The authority boundary, before any effect or mutation is allowed to land.
records.plain_meaning: Holding a credential, a role name, or being inside a 'trusted' session does not by itself give permission to make a change. Permission is worked out fresh for each specific action, from the rules and the current situation.
records.refuses: Treating a standing credential, role name, admin phrasing, trusted session, or tool availability as authority on its own.
records.requires: Deriving authorization from dereferenced proof, policy, and world state, with tool effects bounded by declared identity, scope, approval, and side-effect records.
records.scope_boundary: Deriving authorization in these components does not make P-4 a witness for AX-3 or raise that axiom's support ceiling; it operationalises the specific obligations that authorization is derived, that standing credentials are insufficient, and that tool effects require declared scope, and inherits no authority beyond them. The worked example is reader illustration and computes no support.
records.significance: It closes the gap where a component that holds broad standing power is talked into using it for someone else, since each action still has to earn its own permission.
records.title: Possession is not permission

analogy.boundary: The serial-number picture shows that identity must match the exact item, not just the label; it does not cover every kind of input drift the rule watches, such as changed source bytes, edited files, or a moved parent. It only illustrates the rule.

maps.analogy: the warranty holds only when the serial number still matches the unit
maps.doctrine: reuse only when the stored content tag matches the current inputs

maps.analogy: trusting the model name on the box instead of the serial
maps.doctrine: a name or label match alone licensing reuse

analogy.text: A warranty repair is only valid for the exact unit whose serial number is on the claim. If a part with a different serial was swapped in, a matching model name on the box does not make the old warranty apply.

records.canonical_statement: A reused result is valid only if it carries the digest of the exact content it was computed from; drift in that content forces a recompute, and a name match alone licenses nothing.
records.common_misread: That a matching name or path means the cached answer is still good. Only a match on the actual content the result was computed from licenses reuse; otherwise the work has to be redone.

counterexample.text: A resumed work-landing attempt reuses an earlier result because the task name matches, even though the parent commit and scoped files have changed underneath it.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings non-secret material in by manifest and digest, so when the source bytes drift the imported copy is recomputed rather than reused under the same name.

formal_reading.reads: A prior result may be reused for an incoming basis only when the result's stored content id equals the digest of that basis and the basis actually resolves; in every other case, recompute.

symbols.meaning: a prior result is served for an incoming basis instead of recomputed
a prior result eligible for reuse
the content basis of the incoming request
the digest over that content basis
the content id the result was stored under
the incoming basis cannot be resolved; bottom, licensing no reuse

records.governs: The reuse boundary, where a prior result is about to be served instead of recomputed.
records.plain_meaning: A saved result can be reused only if it is tagged with the exact inputs it was made from, and those inputs still match. If the inputs have changed, you must redo the work; a matching name or label alone is never enough.
records.refuses: Serving a cached result keyed on a name, path, or label after the content behind it has drifted.
records.requires: A declared content basis carried as a digest with the result, equal-basis reuse only, and a recompute or refusal the moment the source digest, scoped files, parent commit, or fixture input drifts.
records.scope_boundary: Carrying a content basis on the reuse paths shown here does not prove reuse is sound everywhere; one freshness-basis mismatch remains a declared open gap, and this card and its example are reader illustration that compute no support for the principle or its grounding axiom.
records.significance: It stops a stale or tampered result being served as fresh just because its name or path still looks familiar after the content underneath has moved.
records.title: Cache by content, not by name

analogy.boundary: This shows why an un-inspected part drags the whole down and why a downstream check cannot upgrade it; it does not establish that the building is actually safe, only that the sign-off chain has no gaps. One full chain of passes is not a promise that nothing was missed.

maps.analogy: the building is fit only if every trade passes; the worst trade decides the whole
maps.doctrine: composite status is the meet of its parts

maps.analogy: a trade with no inspection on file counts as failed, not as quietly fine
maps.doctrine: a part with no evidence defaults to blocked

maps.analogy: a later walk-through can downgrade the building but cannot grant occupancy without a fresh passing inspection
maps.doctrine: a later stage may lower status but not raise it without a new derivation

analogy.text: A building cannot be signed off for occupancy until every trade (wiring, plumbing, fire safety) has passed; if the fire inspection was never done, the building stays unfit, and a later office walk-through can flag a new fault but cannot grant occupancy on its own.

records.canonical_statement: A composite is only as trusted as its weakest part; missing evidence defaults to blocked, and a later stage can lower status but never raise it without a new derivation.
records.common_misread: That an absent or unchecked part is harmless and can be treated as a pass, or that a downstream view can promote something that was blocked underneath it.

counterexample.text: A status board shows a component as green because no scan reported a problem, treating an absent check as a pass and letting a downstream summary stand in for evidence that was never produced.

example.text: The formal-math readiness gate computes its verdict as the meet of its inputs, so when the corpus or a required check is absent it reports blocked rather than ready, and a later projection cannot lift that boundary to passing.

formal_reading.reads: A composite's status is the meet of its parts' statuses; every part with no evidence sits at blocked; and a later status may exceed the earlier one only when some fresh derivation establishes the rise.

a composite whose status is being computed
