records.governs: The status boundary, where a composite verdict is assembled from parts and absence is tempted to read as pass.
records.plain_meaning: A finished thing is only as sound as its weakest part. If one part has no evidence behind it, the whole is treated as not-ready, not assumed fine. A later step can downgrade that verdict but cannot lift it unless fresh work earns the rise.
records.refuses: A silent default pass: letting missing evidence, an absent scan, or a downstream view lift a blocked part to green without a fresh derivation.
records.requires: Taking the composite status as the meet of its parts, defaulting any part with no evidence to blocked, and demanding a new derivation before any later stage raises a status.
records.scope_boundary: Failing closed on missing evidence does not prove any part is correct or that the composite is complete; it only fixes the default so absence lands at blocked rather than passing, and the example here illustrates that default without computing support for the principle or its grounding axiom.
records.significance: It means a missing check or skipped scan cannot quietly pass as approval, so silence has a cost instead of being read as a clean result.
records.title: Status fails closed

analogy.boundary: This shows why a blank result outside the indexed shelves means unknown, not absent; it does not establish that the un-indexed shelves are empty or full, only that the catalogue makes no claim about them. A clean catalogue is not a statement that the whole library was checked.

maps.analogy: the catalogue speaks only for the shelves it has indexed, and those shelves are listed
maps.doctrine: coverage holds only over a declared finite domain

maps.analogy: a title missing from the catalogue is not-yet-indexed, not proof the library lacks it
maps.doctrine: items outside the domain are unknown, not proven-absent

maps.analogy: an empty search result means this catalogue did not find it, not that it cannot exist anywhere
maps.doctrine: absence of a finding is not a claim of safety or completeness

analogy.text: A library catalogue lists the shelves it has actually indexed; a book not in the catalogue might exist on an un-indexed shelf, so the catalogue saying nothing about it means not yet checked, not confirmed absent.

records.canonical_statement: A coverage report names declared domains and the gaps it actually found; it may not call the unmapped space safe, complete, exhausted, or irrelevant.
records.common_misread: That a report finding no problems means the unchecked area is clear, complete, or safe, rather than simply unexamined.

counterexample.text: A report scans a handful of components, finds nothing, and prints the whole area as clear, treating untouched space as evidence that nothing is wrong there.

example.text: The self-ignorance coverage ledger records what was checked, names the gaps it found as open items, and keeps a case that fails when missing coverage is read as a clean result, so a blank region cannot be reported as cleared.

formal_reading.reads: If a report concludes coverage over a domain, that domain must be declared and finite; for anything outside it the report's status is unknown, and unknown is not the same as proven-absent.

symbols.meaning: the report concludes coverage over a domain
a coverage report
the declared finite domain it covers
the domain is explicitly declared and finite
an item, possibly outside the declared domain
what the report says about an item
the coverage tag for anything outside the declared domain
the bottom of the evidence order, i.e. proven-absent or false

records.governs: The coverage-and-completeness boundary, where a report is tempted to read its own silence as a clean result.
records.plain_meaning: A coverage report can only speak about the area it actually checked, and must name the area and the gaps it found. Anything outside that area is unknown, not declared safe, complete, or irrelevant.
records.refuses: Converting absence of a finding into a claim that the unmapped space is safe, complete, exhausted, or irrelevant.
records.requires: Naming the declared finite domain and the gaps actually found, and marking everything outside that domain unknown rather than safe, complete, or exhausted.
records.scope_boundary: Marking the unmapped space unknown does not begin to map it. The gap stays open and the report stays bounded to its declared domain; this principle inherits no authority beyond the specific obligations it operationalises, and the example illustrates the rule without counting as support for it.
records.significance: It stops a tidy report with no findings from being read as everything is fine, when all it really showed is that nothing was looked at outside its declared scope.
records.title: Track known unknowns without claiming the unknown is mapped

analogy.boundary: This shows why a refusal that names its reason beats a fabricated answer; it does not establish that the refusal itself is correct or that the request was valid, only that an unfounded answer is withheld. A returned refusal is information, not a verdict on the underlying request.

maps.analogy: the pharmacist dispenses only when the prescription is complete and valid
maps.doctrine: return an Ok result only when the precondition holds

maps.analogy: an incomplete prescription comes back with the specific missing detail noted on it
maps.doctrine: when the precondition fails, return a typed refusal carrying its reason and evidence

maps.analogy: the pharmacist does not invent a dose to avoid an empty-handed patient
maps.doctrine: do not emit a meaningless value just to keep a green path

analogy.text: A pharmacist who is missing a required detail on a prescription hands it back with the reason written on it, instead of guessing a dose just to send the patient away with a filled bottle.

records.canonical_statement: When a precondition fails or a dependency is missing, return a typed refusal carrying its reason and evidence, never a silent number that keeps the path green.
records.common_misread: That returning some number is always better than returning nothing, even when the basis for that number does not exist.

counterexample.text: A readiness gate reports a corpus as available and prints a green status while the underlying library is missing, turning an absent dependency into a silent overclaim.

example.text: The finance forecast evaluation spine returns a typed refusal when a forecast horizon reaches or exceeds the sample size or when the statistics dependency is unavailable, rather than reporting a loss differential the data cannot support.

formal_reading.reads: An Ok result certifies that the precondition held: a value may come out only when the computation had grounds to run. And when the precondition fails, the computation returns a typed refusal carrying some reason and its evidence, never a bare value.

symbols.meaning: evaluating the partial computation g on input x
a partial computation that can fail
the input to g
an admissible result value y
the declared precondition for running g on input x
the precondition does not hold
a typed refusal carrying reason r and evidence \epsilon

records.governs: The result boundary, where a computation that cannot proceed is tempted to return a number anyway.
records.plain_meaning: When a step cannot be done properly because something needed is missing, it should hand back a clear refusal that says why and shows the basis, not a made-up answer that lets things look finished.
records.refuses: Emitting a meaningless statistic, proof authority, safety verdict, or readiness status to keep a green path when the basis does not admit one.
records.requires: Returning an Ok result only under a declared precondition, and a refusal carrying a reason and evidence when the precondition fails or a dependency is absent.
records.scope_boundary: Returning a typed refusal in these cases does not make P-8 a witness for AX-7 or prove refusal-correctness across every partial computation. It operationalises the specific grounding obligations and inherits no authority beyond the bounded cases that actually refuse.
records.significance: It keeps a system from emitting a forecast past its data, a confidence figure with nothing behind it, or a ready flag while a needed part is missing, all just to avoid showing red.
records.title: Refuse inadmissible computations with typed reasons

analogy.boundary: This shows why a label applied only at the end is weaker than a record carried the whole way; it does not establish that an origin seal guarantees the contents are correct, only that an end-of-line sticker proves less than a continuous record. A carried log is not the same as the goods being verified.

maps.analogy: the parcel carries its origin-stamped custody log through every handoff
maps.doctrine: a value carries its provenance class across the boundary

maps.analogy: the tamper seal is applied at origin and travels intact, not pressed on at the final dock
maps.doctrine: the scope limit is carried, not stamped at the endpoint

maps.analogy: a sticker added only at the loading dock has to be logged as a door-label, not a carried seal
maps.doctrine: an endpoint-only label must be recorded as merely declared

analogy.text: A shipped parcel carries a chain-of-custody log and a tamper seal applied at origin; if a clean sticker was simply slapped on at the loading dock at the end, the log must note that, because a sticker at the door is not the same as an unbroken seal carried the whole route.

records.canonical_statement: A value crossing any boundary must carry its provenance class and scope limit; if a crossing is only declared or labelled at the endpoint, say so rather than treating it as propagated.
records.common_misread: That a clean label at the final destination is enough, regardless of whether that label actually accompanied the value across the earlier boundaries.

counterexample.text: A public page prints a value lifted from a private-adjacent source because the final copy step looked clean, with no provenance carried across the copy.

example.text: The prompt-injection replay carries the information-flow policy with the data, so untrusted instructions arriving at a tool call are caught at the boundary instead of being trusted for having reached a trusted endpoint.

formal_reading.reads: For any value x crossing into a sink s, either x's provenance sits within the sink's policy and its scope limit is carried across, or the crossing is explicitly recorded as only declared.

the value crosses into the sink

the provenance class carried with x

records.governs: Every boundary a value crosses, from imported source through to a public or privileged sink.
records.plain_meaning: A value carries a record of where it came from and how far it is allowed to be used, and that record must travel with it across every handoff. If something was only labelled clean at the final door, that has to be stated, not treated as if the label rode along the whole way.
records.refuses: Reading a clean endpoint label as proof when the label was stamped at the door rather than carried across the boundary.
records.requires: A provenance class and scope limit carried with the value across the crossing, and an explicit record when a flow is only declared or endpoint-labelled.
records.scope_boundary: P-9 guides these flows only through source-derived edges; it is not a witness and does not raise the support ceiling of its grounding axiom. Catching labelled flows at named boundaries does not prove non-interference across the whole graph: general propagation is bounded by what is actually traced, and that debt is recorded rather than hidden.
records.significance: It lets a clean-looking final copy still be treated as unverified, so trust depends on where a value came from and what was done to it, not on how it looks at the last step.
records.title: Preserve provenance across every boundary

analogy.boundary: The wire picture shows that a single transfer can be recorded, reversed, and protected from a race; it does not show the bank's whole system is sound, and one clean transfer does not establish that every account or every transfer is safe.

maps.analogy: the wire being treated as sent
maps.doctrine: the effect being treated as landed

maps.analogy: the entry written into the bank's permanent ledger
maps.doctrine: the durable ordered record

maps.analogy: an attached reversal/chargeback path, or a stated point where it can no longer be pulled back
maps.doctrine: a compensator, or a declared irreversible boundary

maps.analogy: confirming the account balance is still the one the clerk started from before applying the change
maps.doctrine: compare-and-swap on the expected parent

maps.analogy: no second clerk touching the same account at the same moment
maps.doctrine: single writer holding the path

analogy.text: A bank wire only counts as sent once the bank writes it into its permanent ledger, attaches a way to reverse or chargeback if needed, and confirms no second clerk moved the same account in the same moment off an out-of-date balance.

records.canonical_statement: A write, release, or import counts as landed only with a durable ordered record, a compensator or declared irreversible boundary, a compare-and-swap on its expected parent, and a single writer on its path.
records.common_misread: Thinking that because an operation reported success it must therefore be safely and fully landed. The most dangerous moment is the gap between two steps, where a lock can release before the permanent record is written.

counterexample.text: A release step reports success after acquiring a lock but before the durable record is written, so a crash in the gap leaves an effect that looks landed with nothing to recompute or compensate it.

example.text: The mission transaction work spine lands a multi-step effect only after the durable record is written, recomputes once the lock releases, and blocks a stale parent or a same-path conflict, so a half-applied write cannot be reported as complete.

formal_reading.reads: An effect lands only if it is durable and declares a compensator or an irreversible boundary; and when it is multi-step, only under a compare-and-swap on its expected parent with at most one writer on its path.

symbols.meaning: the effect e is treated as landed
an effect

records.governs: The effect boundary, where an operation is about to be treated as landed and irreversible.
records.plain_meaning: An action only counts as done when there is a permanent written record of it, a clear way to undo it (or a stated point of no return), and a check that no two people changed the same thing at once on outdated information. Running successfully is not the same as having landed.
records.refuses: Treating an operation as landed with the lock released before the durable record, a stale parent, or a second writer on the same path.
records.requires: An ordered durable record, a compensator or declared irreversible boundary, a compare-and-swap on the expected parent, and a single-writer hold on the path before the effect counts as landed.
records.scope_boundary: Ordering one transaction and guarding one path does not prove the whole system is crash-safe or serialisable. P-10 operationalises specific AX-9 obligations on the effects it covers; it is not a witness for the axiom and inherits no authority beyond those obligations, and a passing guard on a named path says nothing about effects it never mediated.
records.significance: Without this, a step that crashes between starting and finishing can look complete while leaving a half-done effect that nobody can recompute or unwind, which is how doubled, lost, or unauthorised changes slip through.
records.title: Do not land effects without compensation

analogy.boundary: The board only shows that a changing value needs a timestamp and a live source to stay honest; it does not show the posted time is correct right now, and seeing a refresh link does not mean the value has actually been recomputed since.

maps.analogy: the departure time shown for a train
maps.doctrine: a live count or readiness signal

maps.analogy: the 'last updated' stamp on the board
maps.doctrine: the read time carried with the value

maps.analogy: the board's link back to the live schedule feed it recomputes from
maps.doctrine: the route that re-derives it from its basis

maps.analogy: treating a time painted on the wall as a fixed past notice, not a live departure time
maps.doctrine: demoting an unbound value to a dated snapshot

analogy.text: A train departure board can post a time only because it shows when it last refreshed and recomputes from the live feed; a time painted permanently on the wall, with no refresh, stops being trustworthy the moment the schedule changes.

records.canonical_statement: A live count, current state, or readiness signal may stand in durable prose only if it carries when it was read and a route that recomputes it; otherwise demote it to a dated snapshot.
records.common_misread: Assuming a confident number in a document is current just because it sounds definite. The point that carries the weight is the route to recount it; without that, the figure is just a cached reading wearing the costume of a fixed fact.

counterexample.text: A page states "47 components" as a flat fact, with no read time and no route to recount, so the number keeps asserting itself unchanged after the underlying set has grown.

example.text: The doctrine fact-claim audit checks that public fact rows state the expected count, point at the live code they were read from, and only cite facts present in the fixture graph, so a volatile number without a recompute basis is blocked rather than printed as standing truth.

formal_reading.reads: A volatile value may stand in durable prose only when it carries a read time, a basis, and a route that can recompute it; otherwise demote it to a dated snapshot or keep it out. Carrying the route makes the number checkable; it does not make it currently fresh.

a volatile value such as a count, current state, route total, CI floor, or readiness signal

the value is stamped with its read time, basis, and recompute route
mark v as a dated snapshot or keep it out of durable prose

records.governs: The temporal boundary, where a live reading is tempted to harden into a standing fact.
records.plain_meaning: A number about something that keeps changing, like a current count or a readiness status, may only sit in a lasting document if it says when it was read and points to a way to work it out again. Otherwise it should be marked as a dated snapshot.
records.refuses: A frozen live count or "current" state asserted in durable prose with no read time and no route to re-derive it.
records.requires: A read time, a basis, and a recompute route carried with any live count or readiness signal, or demotion to a dated snapshot when one is missing.
records.scope_boundary: Binding a number to a refresh route does not make the principle a witness for AX-10, and it does not prove the cited value is currently fresh; it operationalises specific freshness obligations and gives the reader a way to recompute, leaving the live reading to be re-derived rather than trusted.
records.significance: A frozen 'current' number quietly drifts from reality as the underlying thing grows or changes, so readers keep trusting yesterday's reading as today's truth with nothing pulling it back into line.
records.title: Bind volatile facts to refresh routes

analogy.boundary: The inspection picture only shows a notice must clear a real check before it can govern; it does not show what the notice says is true, that the room is actually well-built, or that the building code itself is complete.

maps.analogy: the 'meets code' notice on the door
maps.doctrine: a doctrine surface the system may treat as a rule

maps.analogy: passing the coded building inspection
maps.doctrine: passing the executable grammar

maps.analogy: the required sign-offs the inspection demands
maps.doctrine: the required fields and result-record obligations

maps.analogy: the notice stating what it does and does not certify
maps.doctrine: the declared anti-claims, its stated bounds

maps.analogy: a nicely worded but uninspected sign that informs but cannot authorise entry
maps.doctrine: prose held at projection, orients but does not govern

analogy.text: A 'this room meets code' notice on a door can only govern who is allowed in after it passes a coded building inspection with all required sign-offs and stated limits; a convincingly worded sign that no inspector ever ran against the code just orients people, it does not authorise entry.

records.canonical_statement: A doctrine surface earns authority only by passing an executable grammar and carrying receipt obligations and anti-claims; prose alone stays a projection that orients but cannot rule.
records.common_misread: Believing that clear, authoritative-sounding writing is enough to make something a binding rule. Passing the form check means the rule is well-formed enough to carry authority, not that its content is correct.

counterexample.text: A persuasive markdown standard is treated as the rule other components must satisfy, even though nothing parses it against a grammar and it carries no result-record obligations or anti-claims.

example.text: The executable doctrine grammar admits a standard as authority-bearing only when it parses under the grammar and carries its required fields, result-record obligations, and anti-claims, so a doctrine surface that is only prose is held at projection instead of being allowed to govern.

formal_reading.reads: A doctrine surface may carry authority only when an executable grammar accepts it and it carries result-record obligations and anti-claims; otherwise its standing is bounded at projection, which orients but does not rule.

symbols.meaning: a doctrine surface (standard, route contract, record)
the authority the surface is allowed to carry
the language of the executable grammar that must accept it
the result-record obligations the surface declares
the stated bounds on what it does not establish
projection standing: orients a reader, does not govern

records.governs: When a doctrine surface is allowed to govern other components rather than only describe them.
records.plain_meaning: A written rule is only allowed to actually govern other parts of the system after it passes a machine check of its form and carries its required parts and its stated limits. Well-written words on their own can guide a reader but cannot rule.
records.refuses: Letting unstructured prose acquire governing authority without passing the executable contract.
records.requires: Grammar membership plus declared required fields, result-record obligations, and anti-claims before a surface carries authority.
records.scope_boundary: Passing the grammar does not make a doctrine surface true, strong, or its doctrine complete, and it does not make this principle a witness for its grounding axiom; it admits the surface as well-formed authority and inherits no support beyond the specific obligations it operationalizes.
records.significance: It stops a persuasive paragraph from being treated as the law other parts must obey, which is exactly where confident but unchecked wording smuggles authority it never earned.
records.title: Make doctrine executable before authoritative

analogy.boundary: The calibration picture only shows the lab's own instruments face the same standard as customer equipment; it does not show the lab's instruments are actually accurate or its results correct, only that its self-checks were not waved through on a lighter rule.

maps.analogy: the lab's own in-house measuring instruments
maps.doctrine: the system's own meta artifacts describing itself

maps.analogy: the calibration standard being applied
maps.doctrine: the evidence, anti-claim, and refusal floor

maps.analogy: the same standard applied to the customers' equipment the lab certifies
maps.doctrine: the same floor applied to external work it audits

maps.analogy: refusing to let the lab sign off its own gauges under a looser rule
maps.doctrine: refusing a meta-artifact exemption

analogy.text: A calibration lab's own measuring instruments have to pass the very same calibration check it certifies for its customers' equipment; the lab does not get to sign off its own gauges under a looser standard than it applies to everyone else's.

records.canonical_statement: Hold the system's own meta artifacts to the same evidence, anti-claim, and refusal floor they impose on everything else; the meta-layer gets no discount.
records.common_misread: Assuming a system describing itself can be taken at its word, or that holding its self-claims to the standard makes the self-praise correct. Clearing the floor only removes the special pleading; it does not make the system complete or ready.

counterexample.text: The system publishes a self-assessment calling its own work verified and complete, in wording that would never clear the evidence floor it applies to the shards it audits.

example.text: The release wording gate runs the same claim-language floor over the system's own public copy that it imposes on any other shard, so overclaiming language in a self-description is blocked at release rather than waved through.

formal_reading.reads: For every meta artifact the system produces about itself and every claim that artifact carries, the claim must clear the same evidence, anti-claim, and refusal floor as an external claim.

symbols.meaning: the system's meta artifacts: its own standards, paper modules, ledgers, routes, generated projections, and release wording
any such meta artifact
a claim carried by the artifact a
the claims that artifact a carries
the evidence, anti-claim, and refusal floor from AX-12
the same floor applied to an external claim

records.governs: The meta boundary, where the system makes claims about itself and is tempted to discount them.
records.plain_meaning: When the system describes or assesses itself, that self-description has to clear exactly the same evidence and honesty standard it forces on everything else. The part that talks about the system gets no easier pass.
records.refuses: A meta-artifact exemption, where the system's own copy gets a lighter floor than the work it audits.
records.requires: Holding standards, paper modules, ledgers, routes, generated views, and release wording to the same evidence, anti-claim, and refusal floor imposed on any other shard.
records.scope_boundary: Applying the floor to the system's own artifacts does not make the system complete, correct, or release-ready, and it does not make this principle a witness for AX-12. It operationalises specific AX-12 obligations and inherits no authority beyond them; the limits remain, stated rather than hidden.
records.significance: Self-referential copy is where overclaiming is easiest to hide, so removing the discount lets the system state its own limits in public without that statement quietly becoming a new unchecked claim.
records.title: Apply the same floor to meta artifacts

analogy.boundary: The two-label picture only shows that both tags must be present together; it does not establish that a fully labelled sample is correct or safe, and it does not capture how a flow tag tightens as data combines with other data.

maps.analogy: the label naming the patient and the draw the sample came from
maps.doctrine: the content basis (which inputs the value was built from)

maps.analogy: the handling tag stating what the sample may be tested for
maps.doctrine: the provenance (where it may flow and how strongly it may be claimed)

maps.analogy: a tube with only one tag is set aside rather than run
maps.doctrine: a value missing either side is not fully routed

analogy.text: A blood sample reaching the lab needs both a label saying which patient and draw it came from and a handling tag saying what it may be tested for. A tube with only one of the two tags is set aside, not run.

records.canonical_statement: A reusable value is fully routed only when it carries both its content basis (which bytes or rows it came from) and its provenance (where it may flow and how strongly it may be claimed); either half alone leaves it underspecified.
records.common_misread: That one good label is enough. Knowing the source does not tell you where the value may travel, and a flow tag with no record of inputs is a tag attached to nothing.

counterexample.text: A reused result record carries a clean provenance label but no content digest, so it keeps being trusted after the source it was built from has changed underneath it.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings non-secret material in by manifest and digest, so the imported copy carries the content basis that detects source drift and the provenance label that bounds where it may flow, both on the same value before anything downstream reuses it.

formal_reading.reads: A value is fully routed only when it carries a non-empty basis and a non-empty provenance, and any drift in its basis forces a recompute.

symbols.meaning: v is fully routed: it carries both basis and provenance and recomputes on drift
a reusable or travelling value (result record, import, work-landing)
the declared bytes or rows v was computed from, as a digest
v's provenance class and scope limit
the basis digest no longer matches its source
v must be re-derived from source rather than reused
absent: the half was never declared

records.governs: The boundary where a value is reused or crosses a flow, between what it was built from and where it may go.
records.plain_meaning: Every piece of data must carry two things at once: a record of exactly which inputs it was built from, and a record of where it is allowed to go and how strongly it can be claimed. If either tag is missing, the data is not ready to be used.
records.refuses: A shard that declares only its basis or only its provenance and is then treated as fully routed.
records.requires: Both a declared content basis whose drift forces recompute and a provenance class with ceiling carried across every boundary, on the same value.
records.scope_boundary: Carrying both halves on the cases it governs does not witness the axioms it grounds, AX-4 and AX-8, or make either one strong. The principle operationalises specific obligations from those axioms and inherits no authority beyond them; the worked example is reader illustration and computes no support.
records.significance: It stops data from being trusted on a partial paper trail, where you know what something is but not where it may go, or where it may go but not what it actually came from.
records.title: Carry basis and provenance together

analogy.boundary: The photocopy picture shows that a view cannot outrank its origin; it does not capture that the view's ceiling is set by several source properties at once, like freshness and provenance, not just by being a copy.

maps.analogy: the signed original contract
maps.doctrine: the source truth

maps.analogy: the photocopy that can be read but cannot overrule the original
maps.doctrine: the projection held at or below the source

maps.analogy: the old copy losing its standing the moment the original is amended
maps.doctrine: recompute or demote when the source weakens

analogy.text: A photocopy of a contract can be read and shared, but it does not outrank the signed original. If the original is amended, the old copy stops being authoritative.

records.canonical_statement: A generated view may expose a source truth but never upgrade it; recompute from source or demote when the basis no longer supports the projection.
records.common_misread: That a clear, confident-looking view must be reliable. Its reliability is capped by the source, however good the view looks.

counterexample.text: A polished summary page is cited as the authority for a number, even after the source it was generated from has drifted underneath it.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings non-secret material in by manifest and digest and never lets the imported copy outrank the original it was projected from.

formal_reading.reads: A projection's ceiling is at most the meet of its source's support, freshness, provenance, and grammar validity; it can expose the source but never upgrade it.

symbols.meaning: the strongest claim the projection may carry
a generated view (doc, markdown summary, route card, paper module)
is at most as strong as, in the authority lattice
the source's evidence support level
the source the projection draws from
the source's freshness, whether its basis is still current
the source's provenance class and scope limit
the source's grammar validity, whether it is in the executable grammar
meet: the weakest of the components

records.governs: The source-and-projection boundary, between a truth and the views drawn from it.
records.plain_meaning: A generated view can show what a source says, but it can never carry more authority than that source. If the source weakens, the view has to be recomputed or marked as lower.
records.refuses: A generated page, card, or summary that claims more authority than the source it summarises.
records.requires: Holding a projection at or below its source's support, freshness, provenance, and grammar, and recomputing or demoting when any slips.
records.scope_boundary: Keeping a projection below its source does not make the source correct. It only prevents the projection from inventing authority the source never had.
records.significance: It stops a polished public page or summary being treated as more trustworthy than the working source it was drawn from.
records.title: Keep projections below source authority

analogy.boundary: The wire picture shows that several conditions must hold together for one transfer; it does not establish that any given transfer is correct, and it does not capture the claimed write scope and landing evidence that the rule also requires.

maps.analogy: the bank checking this transfer against your approved limits, not just that you are a signatory
maps.doctrine: proof-derived permission, not standing access

maps.analogy: the account still showing the balance you expected before the wire goes out
maps.doctrine: current parent state matching what was expected

maps.analogy: a reversal path if the transfer turns out to be wrong
maps.doctrine: compensation, a way back from the effect

analogy.text: A bank wire is not sent just because you are a signatory. The transfer goes through only when the amount is within your approved limit, the account still shows the balance you expected, and there is a way to reverse it if it was wrong.

records.canonical_statement: A write is authorized only as a bundle bound to this transaction: derived permission, declared scope, the parent state it expected, a compensator or boundary, and landing evidence, never standing identity.
records.common_misread: That having access means you are authorized to change something. Access is one input; permission still has to be worked out for this exact change, against the state you actually find.

counterexample.text: A write is allowed because the agent already holds a token and the file is reachable, landed against whatever the head is now, with no declared scope and no compensator.

example.text: The proof-derived mutation authorization component admits a change only when the authority is derived from policy and proof references for that effect, and rejects a write that leans on a standing credential, so permission is computed per transaction rather than possessed.

formal_reading.reads: A mutation m is authorized within transaction t exactly when its permission is derived rather than possessed, its effect falls inside the transaction's declared write scope, the observed parent state equals the parent the write expected, a compensator or declared irreversible boundary is present, and landing evidence exists for this transaction.

symbols.meaning: mutation m is authorized within transaction t
a proposed mutation (write effect)
the transaction the write is bound to
permission derived from policy, proof, and world state, not a standing credential
the write effect m would apply
the declared write scope of the transaction
the parent state actually observed for the transaction
the parent state the write expected (compare-and-swap base)
a declared compensator or irreversible boundary
evidence that the effect actually landed for this transaction

records.governs: The authorization boundary, where a write is about to be allowed against a file or record.
records.plain_meaning: Being allowed to change something is not one thing, like having access to a file. It is several conditions holding together for this exact change: worked-out permission, the agreed limits of what you may touch, the current state still matching what you expected, a way to undo, and a record that it landed.
records.refuses: Treating reach or a standing credential as authority, or writing on whatever the current state is without an expected parent, scope, or way to compensate.
records.requires: Binding the write to this transaction as derived permission, declared scope, expected parent state, a compensator or boundary, and landing evidence together.
records.scope_boundary: Binding one write to its transaction scope does not make P-16 a witness for AX-3 or AX-9, and the example does not establish that every write path in the system carries the full bundle; it operationalises specific grounding obligations and inherits no authority beyond them.
records.significance: It closes the gap where someone with broad standing access is treated as authorized to make a change, even though nothing checked the specific change against its scope, the current state, and a way to undo it.
records.title: Bind authority to transaction scope

analogy.boundary: The booking picture only shows that a connection must trace to one exact entry, not an approximate one; it does not establish that the entry itself is right, and it does not capture every check a real edge addition runs.

maps.analogy: the exact scheduled service for that date and route
maps.doctrine: the unique source row that names the relation

maps.analogy: a similar flight number or a timetable that lists one more service is not the booking
maps.doctrine: a nearby repeated key or count delta is not a substitute

maps.analogy: the specific booking the connecting seat is attached to
maps.doctrine: the target id and builder route the edge will feed

analogy.text: Before adding a connecting flight to a booking, the agent has to point to the exact scheduled service for that date and route. A flight that merely has a similar number, or the fact that the timetable now lists one more service, does not justify booking the seat.

records.canonical_statement: A lattice edge in a repeated registry is admitted only against the unique source row, target id, and builder route that consume it; a projection, count delta, or repeated key is no basis.
records.common_misread: That a close match or a count that moved confirms the right connection. Only the exact entry that names the relationship does; everything else is a guess that happens to look right.

counterexample.text: A builder adds a lattice edge because the generated registry now reports one more neighbour, treating the count delta as the basis rather than finding the source row that would justify the edge.

example.text: The source-import protocol brings each relation in against a named manifest row and digest and writes it under a declared boundary, so an edge lands only where a current source row carries it rather than where a count or a similar key suggests it.

formal_reading.reads: An edge mutation is admitted only when exactly one source row carries it, that row's content basis is declared, the mutation writes against the row's current parent state, and the resulting edge is grammar-admissible.

symbols.meaning: an edge is added to or removed from a registry of look-alike rows
the lattice edge being added or removed
exactly one source row
the source row that carries the edge
the row's content basis is declared and recomputed on drift (AX-4)
the parent state the writer commits the edge against
the row's current compare-and-swap parent state under a single writer (AX-9)
the resulting edge is admissible under the executable grammar (AX-11)

records.governs: The mutation boundary, where an edge enters or leaves a registry of look-alike rows.
records.plain_meaning: Before adding or removing a connection in a list of relationships, you must point to the exact source entry that names that relationship and the place that will use it. A nearby similar entry, or a count that went up by one, is not good enough to act on.
records.refuses: Anchoring an edge to a generated projection, a count delta, or a nearby repeated key instead of the row that owns it.
records.requires: A unique source row with a declared content basis, a mutation scoped to that row's parent state under a single writer, and grammar admissibility for the resulting edge.
records.scope_boundary: Anchoring a mutation to its source row does not make the edge itself correct, nor does it make this principle a witness for AX-4, AX-9, or AX-11; it operationalises specific obligations of those axioms and inherits no authority beyond them. The example illustrates the rule for a reader and never counts as support that the obligations are covered.
records.significance: In a list where many entries look alike, acting on a near-match or a changed total quietly wires up the wrong relationship, and the mistake is hard to find later.
records.title: Anchor graph mutations to unique source rows

analogy.boundary: The bylaw picture shows that authority needs all the steps to land together before it counts; it does not establish that an enacted rule is wise or correct, and it does not capture the blocked-record case where a partial item is explicitly held without losing its place.

maps.analogy: the bylaw being voted, signed, registered, and status-changed in one sitting
maps.doctrine: the required pieces landing in one governed transaction

maps.analogy: a tabled draft is still a proposal, not the rule
maps.doctrine: a staged item is not yet active authority

maps.analogy: a draft that passed some steps but was never recorded is held over, not enacted
maps.doctrine: partial admission stays residual pressure

analogy.text: A new bylaw is not in force the moment someone tables a draft. It takes effect only when it is voted in, signed, recorded in the register, and its status is formally changed in one sitting. A draft circulating in the room is still a proposal, not the rule.

records.canonical_statement: A staged law, standard, component, or view becomes active authority only when its owner boundary, source row, generated parity, validation record, and status change all land in one governed transaction; partial admission stays pressure.
records.common_misread: That once the content is drafted and looks complete, it is in force. The content is only in force when ownership, source, generated copy, check, and status change all land together.

counterexample.text: A generated card is treated as live the moment it renders, while the source it was built from is still held dirty by another owner and has not landed, so the basis under the card can still move.

example.text: The mission transaction spine lands a multi-step change as one saga with a compare-and-swap parent and a single-writer constraint, so a staged item counts as active only after the whole transaction commits rather than at the first partial step.

formal_reading.reads: A work item carries active authority only when every required dependency has landed under a single transaction; otherwise it stays as residual pressure or blocked.

symbols.meaning: x carries active authority
a work item
the meet taken over the required dependencies
a required dependency
the dependencies x requires
dependency c has landed in transaction tau
the activating transaction
top: fully landed
a single transaction covers the fan-in
residual pressure: not yet active
x is blocked

records.governs: The activation boundary, where a staged change is tempted to count as authority before all of it has landed.
records.plain_meaning: Something new does not start carrying authority just because a draft of it has appeared. All the required pieces, who owns it, the exact source entry, the matching generated copy, a passing check, and the recorded change of status, have to land together in one step. Until then it is still pending, not in force.
records.refuses: Treating a partially admitted change, or a view built on a source another owner still holds dirty, as active authority.
records.requires: Owner boundary, source row, generated parity, validation record, and status change all landing in one single-writer governed transaction, or an explicit blocked record that holds the frontier.
records.scope_boundary: Requiring the components to fan in before activation does not prove the staged change correct, complete, or safe; it only fixes when the change is allowed to count as authority. This object and its example are reader illustration and never compute support for the axioms that ground the principle.
records.significance: It stops a half-finished draft from being treated as the live rule, where some steps are done and the rest are assumed, so nobody is acting on something that never actually took effect.
records.title: Require fan-in before activation

analogy.boundary: The transit-map picture shows that a connection needs a real service, not nearness; it does not capture the typed record the gap carries while it waits, which is more than a blank.

maps.analogy: a known gap on the map, marked but not yet a line
maps.doctrine: the missing relation as typed residual pressure

maps.analogy: the line drawn only when a real, scheduled service connects the stations
maps.doctrine: the edge admitted only when a source names it and the target resolves

analogy.text: A transit map does not draw a line between two stations just because they sit near each other. The line goes in only when a real service runs it.

records.canonical_statement: A missing relation is typed pressure, not an edge; it becomes an edge only when the current source row names it and the target resolves.
records.common_misread: That a plausible-looking connection is a real one. Nearness or a shared name is pressure to check, not a link.

counterexample.text: A builder adds a lattice edge because two ids share a basename, turning a naming coincidence into a claimed relationship.

example.text: The drift control room reports where a projection has wandered from its source as a typed pressure to re-derive, rather than quietly rewriting the edge to make the card look complete.

formal_reading.reads: An edge is admitted only when a current source row names it and its target resolves under the builder; naming alone does not force admission, and anything short of that is not absence but typed residual pressure in R, carrying its declared domain, evidence floor, and anti-claim until a source row resolves it.

symbols.meaning: a candidate relation
the admitted edges
a current source row names the relation
the target resolves under the builder
the target the relation points at
the typed residual-pressure set: pressure, not an edge yet
the residual carries a declared domain, source row, target, fillability, evidence floor, and anti-claim

records.governs: The time-and-coverage boundary, where the map is tempted to fill a gap it cannot yet justify.
records.plain_meaning: A missing link in the map is not an empty space to fill in; it is a noted gap with a type. You only draw the link once a current source actually names it and the target really exists.
records.refuses: Promoting a guess, a basename match, or a generated hint into an edge, in either direction between doctrine and the system.
records.requires: Classifying a missing neighbour as typed pressure with a declared domain, source row, target resolution, and evidence floor before any wiring.
records.scope_boundary: Classifying a residual does not resolve it. The gap stays open and computable until a source row names the relation; the classification just stops it from being faked.
records.significance: It keeps the system's own map honest as it grows, instead of inventing connections that make a diagram look complete.
records.title: Classify residual pressure before wiring

analogy.boundary: The medicine-clearance picture only shows that the supporting records must be attached before use; it does not establish that the underlying tests were good, and it does not capture that a readable leaflet, however well written, never raises what the medicine is cleared to do.

maps.analogy: attaching the test results before the medicine is cleared
maps.doctrine: binding validator results to the record before it is authoritative

maps.analogy: the list of what was tested and the known side effects travelling with the medicine
maps.doctrine: evidence refs and stated omissions on the record

maps.analogy: the approved use that limits what the medicine may be dispensed for
maps.doctrine: the authority ceiling bound on the record

analogy.text: A medicine is not cleared for the shelf just because a printed information leaflet for it exists. The test results, the list of what was tested, the known side effects, and the approved use all have to be attached to that medicine before it can be dispensed.

records.canonical_statement: A record's existence as JSON, markdown, or public copy is not its authority; it earns authority only once it carries its result records, evidence refs, omissions, anti-claims, and scope limits.
records.common_misread: That a clear, complete-looking record is therefore authoritative. The readable copy is a projection; authority comes only from the checks and limits bound to the record, not from how it reads.

counterexample.text: A generated doctrine card is cited as the current authority for a rule because the JSON exists and the heading reads confidently, with no validator result records, omissions, or scope limit bound behind it.

example.text: The release wording gate runs the same claim-language floor over the system's own doctrine copy that it imposes on any other shard, so a record's public line cannot carry authority its bound checks do not support.

formal_reading.reads: A doctrine record may be read as current authority only when it carries its bound result records, evidence refs, declared omissions, anti-claims, and scope limit; missing any of these, it stays a projection below its source.

symbols.meaning: reading the record back as current substrate authority
a doctrine record: its JSON, markdown, routing edge, or public copy
the validator result records are bound on the record (AX-11)
the evidence refs bound on the record
the declared omissions bound on the record
the anti-claims bound on the record
the scope limit, the authority ceiling the record may carry (AX-12)

records.governs: The record-and-authority boundary, between a doctrine record existing and being read back as current authority.
records.plain_meaning: A record does not become the trusted version just because a readable copy of it exists. The evidence that its checks passed, the references to its evidence, what it leaves out, and how far it is allowed to claim all have to be attached to the record first.
records.refuses: Reading a record's JSON, markdown, routing edge, or public copy as authoritative on the strength of its existence, before the result records are bound.
records.requires: Binding the validator result records, evidence refs, omissions, anti-claims, and scope limit on a record before any of its projections is treated as current authority.
records.scope_boundary: Binding result records to a doctrine record does not make the record correct, witnessed, or complete; it only stops the record from carrying authority its bound checks do not support. The example illustrates the rule and never counts as support for it.
records.significance: It stops a nicely formatted record from being treated as the trusted source when the checks, evidence, and limits that would back it have not actually been attached.
records.title: Bind receipts before record authority

source_of_record.source_authority_boundary: Hand-authored reader enrichment for the public doctrine cards (deep reading, formal statement, worked example, counterexample, enforcement routes, does-not-prove bound). This file is reader content, NOT support evidence: the axiom support-cover validator does not read it, examples here never compute support, and a generated page may expose this content but never raise the authority of the source it explains. Source of record for the formal_clause itself remains core/axiom_organ_routing.json (axioms), PRINCIPLES.md (principles), ANTI_PRINCIPLES.md (anti-principles); the latex here is a reviewed reader rendering of that clause, not a competing source.
